profile.name: Wilbert B. Smith
roles: Superintendent of Radio Regulations Engineering - Canadian Department of Transport
profile.clearance: Top Secret (Canadian government; active during Project Magnet)
profile.summary: Senior Canadian government radio engineer who authored the classified 1950 Smith Memorandum - one of the most significant government documents in UAP research - documenting that U.S. officials had privately told him flying saucers were classified above the H-bomb and that a small group under Vannevar Bush was analyzing recovered hardware. Smith subsequently founded and directed Project Magnet, Canada's official government UFO investigation program (1950-1954). His memo was declassified by the Canadian government in 1979 and later partially corroborated when Dr. Robert Sarbacher confirmed in a 1983 letter that Smith's account of their 1950 conversation was accurate.
education: B.Sc., Electrical Engineering, University of British Columbia, 1933
M.Sc., Electrical Engineering, University of British Columbia, 1934
early_career: Joined the Canadian Department of Transport in 1939 as a radio engineer; rose to Superintendent of Radio Regulations Engineering
Managed Canada's national radio monitoring and broadcast engineering infrastructure during and after World War II
Became personally interested in UAP reports in the late 1940s through analysis of anomalous radio signal data

key_events.event: Joined the Canadian Department of Transport as a senior radio engineer; spent the following two decades as Superintendent of Radio Regulations Engineering, overseeing Canada's national electromagnetic monitoring infrastructure.

Authored the classified 'Smith Memorandum' to the Canadian Controller of Telecommunications. Drawing on conversations with U.S. officials including Dr. Robert Sarbacher, the memo stated that flying saucers are real, are operated by intelligent beings, use technology that exploits the 'binding force' of matter, and are classified above the H-bomb as the most secret subject in the United States government. Smith requested formal government sanction for a UAP investigation program.

Project Magnet formally approved by the Canadian Department of Transport - Canada's official government UAP investigation program and one of the first formal national programs outside the United States. Smith directed it as a secondary assignment alongside his regular radio engineering duties.

Project Second Storey established by the Canadian government as a government-wide advisory committee on UAP, with Smith as a principal scientific figure. The committee reviewed Canadian UAP sighting reports and coordinated with defense and intelligence officials.

The Project Magnet monitoring station at Shirley's Bay (near Ottawa) detected a significant anomalous electromagnetic disturbance that could not be attributed to known aircraft or natural phenomena. Shortly thereafter, the Department of Transport downgraded Project Magnet from an official program to an informal study, ending its formal government status.

Continued private research into what he called 'binding forces' - theoretical electromagnetic principles he believed explained UAP propulsion. Gave public lectures and wrote 'The Boys from Topside,' an account of alleged communications with non-human intelligence - a departure that diminished his credibility among mainstream researchers.

Died of cancer in Ottawa at age 52. His 1950 memorandum was declassified by the Canadian government in 1979. In 1983, Dr. Robert Sarbacher confirmed in a letter to UAP researcher William Steinman that his 1950 statements to Smith - about UAP classification and recovered hardware - were accurate.

claims.claim: Flying saucers are classified by the U.S. government at a level higher than the hydrogen bomb - the most secret subject in the U.S. government
claims.explanation: Smith documented this as what he was told by Dr. Robert Sarbacher, a Defense Department consultant, in 1950. The claim was classified at the time and Smith treated it as secondhand information from a credible source. In 1983, Sarbacher wrote to researcher William Steinman confirming that he had told Smith these things and that his statements were accurate - that UFOs were real, that hardware had been recovered, and that the subject was classified at a high level. The 'partially-verified' status reflects Sarbacher's 1983 written corroboration of the conversation, though full independent confirmation of the classification claim remains unavailable.

claims.claim: A small group operating under Dr. Vannevar Bush was analyzing recovered UAP hardware in 1950
claims.explanation: Smith's memo named Vannevar Bush specifically as leading the group working on recovered UAP materials. Sarbacher's 1983 letter confirmed the general substance of their conversation but was less specific about the Bush connection. No declassified government document has confirmed a Bush-led UAP hardware analysis group, though Bush's role as a wartime science administrator and his involvement in classified programs of that era is well-documented. The claim remains unverified but has been taken seriously by researchers given Sarbacher's partial corroboration.

supporting: Senior government engineer with M.Sc. credentials and no known history of fabrication during his professional career

The Smith Memorandum was a classified internal government document - not a public claim - written for his superiors requesting sanction for official research; the context argues against self-serving fabrication
The Canadian government's 1979 declassification of the memo is a formal institutional act that authenticates the document's existence and authorship
Dr. Robert Sarbacher's 1983 written confirmation that his 1950 statements to Smith were accurate is independent corroboration by a named, credentialed source
Project Magnet received official Canadian government sanction - meaning his superiors found his investigation credible enough to authorize
His professional role in radio engineering gave him legitimate access to U.S. government scientific contacts in Washington, making the Sarbacher meetings plausible
contradicting: His later 'Boys from Topside' writings about contactee communications with non-human intelligence lack any evidentiary basis and significantly complicate his credibility
The core UAP classification claims are secondhand - he documented what others told him, not his own direct access to classified programs
Project Magnet's 1954 anomalous detection at Shirley's Bay was never independently verified or published in a peer-reviewed context
The Vannevar Bush attribution in the memo has never been corroborated by declassified records, and Bush himself never publicly commented on UAP
No other Canadian or American government official from the 1950 period has corroborated the substance of the Sarbacher briefing on record
credibility.overall: Smith is a credible and significant historical figure in UAP research primarily because of the 1950 memo - a classified government document with institutional authentication and partial independent corroboration via Sarbacher's 1983 letter. His engineering credentials and the official sanction of Project Magnet strengthen the institutional context of his claims. However, his later contactee writings introduce legitimate credibility concerns, and the core classified claims remain secondhand. He is best assessed as a credible conduit for significant secondhand information from 1950, with appropriate limits on the weight assigned to his later independent claims.

associated_people.name: Stanton Friedman
associated_people.relationship: Friedman was instrumental in researching, publicizing, and contextualizing the Smith Memorandum after its 1979 declassification; Smith's memo became one of the central documents in Friedman's MJ-12 and government secrecy research.

associated_people.name: Donald Keyhoe
associated_people.relationship: Contemporaneous figures in 1950s UAP advocacy; Keyhoe's NICAP and Smith's Project Magnet operated in parallel as the two most institutionally credible UAP investigations of the era.

disclosures.title: Smith Memorandum - Classified Internal Memo to Controller of Telecommunications
disclosures.notes: The primary document of Smith's UAP legacy. A classified internal memo requesting government sanction for UAP research, documenting conversations with U.S. officials including Dr. Sarbacher about UAP classification level and recovered hardware. Declassified by the Canadian government in 1979 and subsequently widely published in UAP research literature.

disclosures.title: The Boys from Topside
disclosures.notes: Accounts of alleged communications with non-human intelligences. Represents Smith's later contactee-oriented work and is the source of significant credibility concerns among researchers who otherwise respect his earlier government documents.

disclosures.title: Project Magnet Report
disclosures.notes: Official summary report of Project Magnet's findings, concluding that UAP phenomena appeared to be real and warranted continued investigation. Released following the program's downgrade in 1954.

sources.title: The Wilbert Smith Memorandum (November 21, 1950) - Original declassified document
sources.notes: Primary source - declassified by Canadian government 1979; the foundational document of Smith's UAP legacy

sources.title: Dr. Robert Sarbacher Letter to William Steinman (November 29, 1983)
sources.notes: Sarbacher's written corroboration that his 1950 statements to Smith about UAP classification were accurate; key independent verification of the Smith Memo's sourcing

sources.title: Project Magnet - Library and Archives Canada
sources.notes: Canadian federal archive records of Project Magnet as an official government program

career_connections.relationship: Friedman researched and publicized the Smith Memorandum after its 1979 declassification; the memo became central to Friedman's MJ-12 and government secrecy work.
Project Magnet (Canada) and Blue Book (U.S.) were parallel official government UAP programs operating simultaneously in the early 1950s.
NICAP and Project Magnet were the two most institutionally credible parallel UAP investigations of the 1950s; Keyhoe and Smith operated in overlapping advocacy space.
